channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Pace of N.I.H. Funding Slows Further in Trump’s Second Year
item.description: The agency has approved far fewer new grants than it did in years past. A renewed effort to screen for disfavored terms and a loss of personnel are contributing.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mueller and Irena Hwang
item.media:credit: Alyssa Schukar for The New York Times
item.media:description: The main campus of the National Institutes of Health, in Bethesda, Md.

item.title: New Gene Therapy Enables Children With a Rare Form of Deafness to Hear
item.description: The treatment, the first of its kind, was approved by the Food and Drug Administration on Thursday. “Our baby was born deaf, and now he can hear,” said one parent.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: Miles was born with otoferlin deafness, a rare form of deafness caused by a mutation in a single gene. But with a new gene therapy, he can hear.

item.title: Dark Skies and Dark Energy Converge in West Texas
item.description: In the Big Bend region, a portal to the early universe is enabled by the largest dark-sky reserve on Earth.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller and Emily Elconin
item.media:credit: Emily Elconin for The New York Times

item.title: Lyrids Meteor Shower: How to Watch, Peak Time and Weather Forecast
item.description: Active since last week, the Lyrids meteor shower is expected to reach peak viewing tonight.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Daniel Reinhardt/DPA, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The Lyrids meteor shower over Burg on the Baltic Sea island of Fehmarn off Germany in 2018.

item.title: Musk’s SpaceX Goals Shift Ahead of Its I.P.O.
item.description: As SpaceX prepares to go public, Mr. Musk has proposed moonshots that differ from the company’s original aim of reaching Mars.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac
item.media:credit: Ben Voldman

item.title: Sightings of Meteors Surge, and Scientists Aren’t Sure Why
item.description: The frequency of fireballs in our planet’s skies seemed to grow in recent months. NASA and other meteor experts can’t agree on what explains it.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews
item.media:credit: Jared Rackley, via Associated Press

item.title: What’s in a Name? For These Snails, Legal Protection
item.description: Scientists are debating the classification of threatened mollusks that an Indigenous community relies on for their way of life.
item.dc:creator: Zoë Lescaze and María José Zavala Balcázar
item.media:credit: María José Zavala Balcázar

item.title: How Bruce the Parrot Landed Atop the Pecking Order, Without a Beak
item.description: The kea gained fame for learning to use a pebble to groom himself. Scientists were astounded by his next innovation.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer

item.title: These Salmon Got High on Cocaine. That Wasn’t the Craziest Part.
item.description: Scientists in Sweden made an unexpected discovery when they exposed the fish to the illegal drug as well as another substance.
item.dc:creator: Annie Roth

item.title: Doug Allan, Polar Cameraman for David Attenborough’s Films, Dies at 74
item.description: He was renowned for his skill at capturing candid scenes of penguins, polar bears and other cold-weather creatures — and for his ability to tolerate extreme discomfort.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: Trump Keeps Talking About Iran’s ‘Nuclear Dust.’ What Is It?
item.description: First of all, it isn’t dust. It’s Iran’s stockpile of near-bomb-grade uranium, which is stored in large canisters about the size of scuba tanks.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Planet Labs, via Reuters
item.media:description: A satellite image from February showing construction on buildings at the Isfahan nuclear site in Iran that were destroyed during U.S. airstrikes last June.

item.title: A New Bureau Will Oversee Both Offshore Drilling and Seabed Mining
item.description: The new federal office will undo a change made after the 2010 Deepwater Horizon oil disaster. Critics say it could reduce environmental oversight.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Frederic J. Brown/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Drilling platforms off California in 2021. The Trump administration said the move would speed approvals of offshore energy and mining projects.

item.title: Sewage Spill Is Over, But Contamination Lingers In Potomac
item.description: Though river monitoring shows bacteria levels have declined, scientists and environmentalists said a full recovery isn’t yet assured.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Michael Noble Jr. for The New York Times

item.title: RFK Jr. Says His Department Advises All Children to Get Measles Vaccine
item.description: Testifying on Capitol Hill, Robert F. Kennedy Jr. continued to back away from his criticism of the measles shot. But he spoke on behalf of his department, not himself.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Robert F. Kennedy Jr., the health secretary, at the Capitol on Tuesday.

item.title: On Earth Day, House Cancels Vote to Narrow Endangered Species Protections
item.description: House leaders abruptly canceled a vote on the measure when support started to look shaky.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Catrin Einhorn
item.media:credit: Jim Urquhart/Reuters
item.media:description: A grizzly bear in Yellowstone National Park in Wyoming last summer. Grizzlies are one of several species whose populations have rebounded in some areas in the past several decades.

item.title: Youth Suicides Declined After Creation of National Hotline
item.description: Suicides among young adults dropped most sharply in states that actively embraced the 988 crisis line, new research has found.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Barry
item.category: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration
Journal of the American Medical Assn
item.media:credit: Brandon Thibodeaux for The New York Times
item.media:description: A crisis line specialist working the phone at a mental health crisis center in Houston in 2022.

item.title: ‘Free Births’ Are a New Pregnancy Trend. Critics Warn About Serious Risks.
item.description: Promoters of free birthing reject any type of medical intervention during pregnancy or delivery. The movement has been trending on social media, but critics warn it poses serious risks.
item.dc:creator: Dawn MacKeen
item.media:credit: Bethany Mollenkof for The New York Times
item.media:description: When Emily Laszlo-Rath found out she was pregnant in 2021, she became intent on a “free birth.” “I just kind of started romanticizing the whole thing of being at home,” she said.

item.title: Does Clothing Recycling Really Work?
item.description: Collection services offer convenience, but most garments are shredded into low-grade stuffing or sent abroad to an uncertain fate.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Cernansky
item.media:credit: Mikayla Whitmore for The New York Times
item.media:description: A sorting facility for Supercircle in North Las Vegas this month. The start-up aims to help the fashion industry use discarded textiles to make new clothes.

item.title: Judge Halts Trump Actions That Have Slowed Renewable Energy
item.description: The Interior Department had imposed restrictions on wind and solar projects across the country, prompting developers to sue.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: David Robert Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: The case centers on actions by the Interior Department that slowed or restricted federal approvals for wind and solar projects on both public and private lands.

item.title: E.P.A., Maryland Sue D.C. Utility Over Potomac River Sewage
item.description: In separate lawsuits, environmental regulators said D.C. Water failed to prevent and contain a massive spill of raw sewage upstream of Washington.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sewage-contaminated water flowing into a creek inside Chesapeake and Ohio Canal National Historical Park near Cabin John, Md., last month.

item.title: Blue Origin’s Failure May Hamstring NASA’s Moon Plans
item.description: The space agency is counting on Jeff Bezos’ company to deliver equipment essential to the next moon landing, only two years away.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:credit: Joe Skipper/Reuters
item.media:description: Blue Origin’s New Glenn rocket lifting off from Cape Canaveral, Fla., on Sunday. While the launch was successful, the deployment of its payload, a communications satellite, was not.

item.title: ‘Earthset’ Is Captured on Video for First Time
item.description: The Earth slips behind the moon in an iPhone video taken by Reid Wiseman during NASA’s Artemis II mission, a sequel to the iconic Earthrise photo taken by Apollo 8.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan

item.title: Desmond Morris, 98, Dies; Zoologist Saw Links Between Humans and Apes
item.description: His immensely popular, and controversial, 1967 book, “The Naked Ape,” argued that ancient genes, shared with apes, shape our lives.
item.dc:creator: Douglas Martin
item.media:credit: Desmond Morris Collection/Universal Images Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Desmond Morris in 1956 with the chimpanzees Congo and Charlie on the show “Zoo Time,” a Granada Television series he hosted about animals at the London Zoo.

item.title: A Humanoid Robot Races to a Record Half-Marathon Finish
item.description: The android won a race featuring robots and humans on Sunday in Beijing, achieving a technological milestone while finishing faster than any person in history.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Kevin Frayer/Getty Images
item.media:description: The 5-foot-5 humanoid robot Lightning, with its approximately 3-foot-long legs striding, showed its form in a half-marathon race on Sunday in Beijing.

item.title: Elaine Ingham, Who Taught That Soil Is Alive, Dies at 73
item.description: A scientist and leader in the organic farming movement, she popularized the “soil food web,” an understanding that soil is a complex realm of microorganisms.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel

item.title: The Help That Many Older Americans Need Most
item.description: With shortages of medical professionals and an aging population, thousands of community health care workers prevent older adults from falling through the cracks.
item.dc:creator: Paula Span
item.media:credit: Michael Hanson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ms. Guzman during a visit to a client’s home recently in The Dalles, Ore.

item.title: Iris Long, Scientific Mentor to AIDS Activists, Dies at 92
item.description: A chemist, she lent her expertise in drug research to ACT UP, an organization known for its street protests, and helped accelerate the approval of H.I.V. and AIDS treatments.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: Amit Forlit, Accused of Hacking Climate Activists, Appears in Court
item.description: Amit Forlit is accused of running a hacking operation that aimed to thwart climate litigation at the behest of a lobbying firm working for an oil giant.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal District Court in New York City. Amit Forlit has been indicted on charges of conspiracy to commit computer hacking, conspiracy to commit wire fraud and wire fraud.

item.title: This Scientist Looked for Ways to Stop Mass Violence
item.description: David Eisenman sought to better understand how to prevent school shootings and other targeted violence, until the Department of Homeland Security halted the research.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times

item.title: How Female Anglerfish Evolved to Have It All
item.description: The deep-sea fish ended up with glowing lures not just to snag meals, but also to attract mates, a new study finds.
item.dc:creator: Kate Golembiewski

item.title: New Treatment Lets 3 Transplant Patients Halt Anti-Rejection Drugs
item.description: Researchers at the University of Pittsburgh gave transplant recipients certain immune cells from their organ donors. It didn’t always work.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Aaron Blum for The New York Times

item.title: How Can I Protect My Car When the Weather Is Scorching?
item.description: Extreme heat is extremely hard on your vehicle, but a few simple precautions can help a lot.
item.dc:creator: Susan Shain
item.media:credit: Apu Gomes/Getty Images
